cases.name: t304
cases.input: Test 304. The link below gives a lovely uncaught exception in Firefox 3.0.2 with Firebug, which prevents using the testbed. Is it a Firefox bug? A Firebug issue? No clue. For now, I have disabled this test by commenting out the link, so that the rest of the testbed continues to work.

content.text: Test 304. The link below gives a lovely uncaught exception in Firefox 3.0.2 with Firebug, which prevents using the testbed. Is it a Firefox bug? A Firebug issue? No clue. For now, I have disabled this test by commenting out the link, so that the rest of the testbed continues to work.

cases.canonical: Test 304. The link below gives a lovely uncaught exception in Firefox 3.0.2 with Firebug, which prevents using the testbed. Is it a Firefox bug? A Firebug issue? No clue. For now, I have disabled this test by commenting out the link, so that the rest of the testbed continues to work.

cases.name: t376
cases.input:
Test 376. Test for a specific bug: the sanitizer has code like 
  for name, value in tag.attrs:
    if name not in attr_whitelist:
      del tag[name]
This is buggy, because it mutates a list while iterating over it. As a result, this buggy code might delete only every other attribute instead of every attribute. It can be exploited by introducing useless attributes.

content.text: Test 376. Test for a specific bug: the sanitizer has code like

This is buggy, because it mutates a list while iterating over it. As a result, this buggy code might delete only every other attribute instead of every attribute. It can be exploited by introducing useless attributes.

cases.name: t378
cases.input: Test 378. Test for malicious HTML inside an image SRC. (This broke one sanitizer, which rewrote links into the text of the page a la lynx. For unrelated reasons, it was a probabilistical break, so I had to repeat it several times to give myself the maximum chance to succeed.)

cases.name: t389
cases.input: Test 389. Test for another common error: the sanitizer tries to check that the URL starts with http:, but forgot to include an anchor in the regexp (the buggy regexp was "(http:|https:)"; it should have been "^(http:|https:)"). clicky clicky

content.text: Test 389. Test for another common error: the sanitizer tries to check that the URL starts with http:, but forgot to include an anchor in the regexp (the buggy regexp was "(http:|https:)"; it should have been "^(http:|https:)"). clicky clicky

cases.canonical: Test 389. Test for another common error: the sanitizer tries to check that the URL starts with http:, but forgot to include an anchor in the regexp (the buggy regexp was "(http:|https:)"; it should have been "^(http:|https:)"). clicky clicky

cases.name: t390
cases.input:
<!DOCTYPE html><HTML><HEAD></HEAD><BODY>

Test 390.  This was a near-miss: I didn't break any sanitizer,
but it was a close call.  It exploits difference between SGML
parsing of comments and what you might expect.  It almost breaks
BeautifulSoup-based sanitizers, but not quite.  The string
below is parsed differently by BeautifulSoup and Firefox:
BeautifulSoup thinks the comment ends just before c,
but Firefox thinks it doesn't end until e.  It fails to break
BeautifulSoup-based parsers, because when BeautifulSoup pretty-prints
the output, it html-escapes the contents of the name attribute.
Unfortunately, the DOCTYPE declaration is essential.

<!--a--b-->c<div name="d-->e<script>alert(390)</script>">

</BODY></HTML>

content.text: Test 390. This was a near-miss: I didn't break any sanitizer, but it was a close call. It exploits difference between SGML parsing of comments and what you might expect. It almost breaks BeautifulSoup-based sanitizers, but not quite. The string below is parsed differently by BeautifulSoup and Firefox: BeautifulSoup thinks the comment ends just before c, but Firefox thinks it doesn't end until e. It fails to break BeautifulSoup-based parsers, because when BeautifulSoup pretty-prints the output, it html-escapes the contents of the name attribute. Unfortunately, the DOCTYPE declaration is essential. c

cases.canonical: Test 390. This was a near-miss: I didn't break any sanitizer, but it was a close call. It exploits difference between SGML parsing of comments and what you might expect. It almost breaks BeautifulSoup-based sanitizers, but not quite. The string below is parsed differently by BeautifulSoup and Firefox: BeautifulSoup thinks the comment ends just before c, but Firefox thinks it doesn't end until e. It fails to break BeautifulSoup-based parsers, because when BeautifulSoup pretty-prints the output, it html-escapes the contents of the name attribute. Unfortunately, the DOCTYPE declaration is essential. c

cases.name: t393
cases.input:
<!DOCTYPE html><HTML><HEAD></HEAD><BODY>

Test 393.  Another one that appears to cause differences in parsing
between BeautifulSoup and Firefox.  BeautifulSoup thinks the script tag
is inside the comment, Firefox thinks it is outside.

<!-- a -- b > c<SCRIPT>alert(393)</SCRIPT> -->d

</BODY></HTML>

content.text: Test 393. Another one that appears to cause differences in parsing between BeautifulSoup and Firefox. BeautifulSoup thinks the script tag is inside the comment, Firefox thinks it is outside. d

cases.canonical: Test 393. Another one that appears to cause differences in parsing between BeautifulSoup and Firefox. BeautifulSoup thinks the script tag is inside the comment, Firefox thinks it is outside. d

cases.name: t403
cases.input:
<HTML><HEAD></HEAD><BODY>

Test 403.  Exploit IE conditional comments.
Should only succeed against IE7 or greater.
Thanks to Thurston Dang for this attack.

<!--[if true]>
    <marquee>Hi from t403</marquee>
    <script>
        alert(403);
    </script>
<![endif]-->



</BODY></HTML>

content.text: Test 403. Exploit IE conditional comments. Should only succeed against IE7 or greater. Thanks to Thurston Dang for this attack.
